findings.title: Ransomware Actors Exploit Unpatched SimpleHelp Remote Monitoring and Management to Compromise Utility Billing Software Provider
findings.summary: Summary The Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) is releasing this advisory in response to ransomware actors leveraging unpatched instances of a vulnerability in SimpleHelp Remote Monitoring and Management (RMM) to compromise customers of a utility billing software provider. This incident reflects a broader pattern of ransomware actors targeting organizations through unpatched versions of SimpleHelp RMM since January 2025. SimpleHelp versions 5.5.7 and earlier contain several vulnerabilities, including CVE-2024-57727 —a path traversal vulnerability. 1 Ransomware actors likely leveraged CVE-2024-57727 to access downstream customers’ unpatched SimpleHelp RMM for disruption of services in double extortion compromises. 1 CISA added CVE-2024-57727 to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) Catalog on Feb. 13, 2025. CISA urges software vendors, downstream customers, and end users to immediately implement the Mitigations listed in this advisory based on confirmed compromise or risk of compromise. Download the PDF version of this report: AA25-163A Ransomware Actors Exploit Unpatched SimpleHelp Remote Monitoring and Management to Compromise Utility Billing Software Provider (PDF, 420.49 KB ) Mitigations CISA recommends organizations implement the mitigations below to respond to emerging ransomware activity exploiting SimpleHelp software. These mitigations align with the Cross-Sector Cybersecurity Performance Goals (CPGs) developed by CISA and the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST). The CPGs provide a minimum set of practices and protections that CISA and NIST recommend all organizations implement. CISA and NIST based the CPGs on existing cybersecurity frameworks and guidance to protect against the most common and impactful threats, tactics, techniques, and procedures. Visit CISA’s CPGs webpage for more information on the CPGs, including additional recommended baseline protections. These mitigations apply to all critical infrastructure

enrichment.technical_details: An unauthenticated path traversal (CWE-22) in SimpleHelp's web application caused by improper sanitization of user-supplied filenames/path components. A remote, unauthenticated attacker sends crafted HTTP requests with ../ sequences (e.g., ../../../../../../etc/passwd) to traverse outside the intended web directory and read or download arbitrary files on the SimpleHelp host. Sensitive materials accessible include SimpleHelp server configuration files containing credentials, API tokens, and hashed user passwords. A Metasploit auxiliary module (auxiliary/scanner/http/simplehelp_toolbox_path_traversal), a public GitHub PoC, and a ProjectDiscovery Nuclei template are all publicly available.
enrichment.affected_products: SimpleHelp Remote Monitoring and Management (RMM) versions 5.5.7 and earlier. The vendor advisory bundles this CVE together with CVE-2024-57726 and CVE-2024-57728.
enrichment.threat_actors: DragonForce ransomware (per CISA AA25-163A). CISA also generically describes 'ransomware actors' leveraging unpatched SimpleHelp instances through a utility billing software provider since January 2025.
enrichment.mitigation: Upgrade to SimpleHelp v5.5.8 or later (or apply the vendor's released patch for v5.4.10 / v5.3.9). After upgrading: rotate all Administrator and Technician passwords, regenerate API tokens, restrict API and login request source IPs, enforce firewall restrictions, and enable Server Event alerts. CISA additionally recommends: isolate or stop any unpatched SimpleHelp server exposed to the internet; hunt for locally created executables with three-letter alphabetic names (e.g., aaa.exe, bbb.exe) dated after January 2025; for ransomware-encrypted hosts, disconnect from the internet, reinstall from clean media, and restore from clean (offline) backups; maintain a daily offline backup cadence; do not expose RDP or other remote services to the public internet; and conduct a risk review of any RMM software on the network.

findings.title: AI threats in the wild: The current state of prompt injections on the web
findings.summary: Posted by Thomas Brunner, Yu-Han Liu, Moni Pande At Google, our Threat Intelligence teams are dedicated to staying ahead of real-world adversarial activity, proactively monitoring emerging threats before they can impact users. Right now, Indirect Prompt Injection (IPI) is a top priority for the security community, anticipating it as a primary attack vector for adversaries to target and compromise AI agents. But while the danger of IPI is widely discussed, are threat actors actually exploiting this vector today – and if so, how? To answer these questions and to uncover real-world abuse, we initiated a broad sweep of the public web to monitor for known indirect prompt injection patterns. This is what we found. The threat of indirect prompt injection Unlike a direct injection where a user "jailbreaks" a chatbot, IPI occurs when an AI system processes content—like a website, email, or document—that contains malicious instructions. When the AI reads this poisoned content, it may silently follow the attacker's commands instead of the user's original intent. This is not a new area of concern for us and Google has been working tirelessly to combat these threats. Our efforts involve cross-functional collaboration between researchers at Google DeepMind (GDM) and defenders like the Google Threat Intelligence Group (GTIG). We have previously detailed our work in this area and researchers have further highlighted the evolving nature of these vulnerabilities. Despite this collective focus, a fundamental question remains: to what degree are real-world malicious actors currently operationalizing these attacks? Proactive monitoring at Google The landscape of IPI on the web There are many channels through which attackers might try to send prompt injections. However, one location is particularly easy to observe - the public web. Here, threat actors may simply seed prompt injections on websites in hope of corrupting AI systems that browse them. Public research confirms these attacks ar

enrichment.technical_details: Indirect Prompt Injection (IPI) is a prompt-injection variant where an attacker embeds adversarial instructions inside content that an LLM/AI agent ingests as data — including web pages, emails, documents, HTML comments, CSS-hidden elements (display:none, font-size:1px, rgba(0,0,0,0)), aria-hidden attributes, meta tags, or README/code comments. When the AI reads the poisoned content, the malicious instructions are silently fused into the model's context, overriding the user's original intent. Documented attack intents include: (1) data exfiltration (API keys, cookies, system prompts); (2) destruction (e.g., 'sudo rm -rf'); (3) financial fraud (embedded PayPal/Stripe links, unauthorized transactions); (4) SEO/influence manipulation of AI summaries; (5) denial-of-service against crawlers (infinite-text streams, refusal-trigger strings); (6) prompt/system-prompt leakage; and (7) RCE pivots when chained with agentic code-execution sinks (Cursor allowlist bypass via backtick/`$(cmd)`, Semantic Kernel eval()/DownloadFileAsync abuse, VS Code .vscode/settings.json tampering). Google's Common Crawl telemetry shows a 32% increase in malicious-category IPI detections between November 2025 and February 2026.
enrichment.affected_products: Google Gemini (Workspace Gemini and Gemini-powered agents), Cursor code editor <1.3 (CVE-2025-54131), Microsoft Semantic Kernel Python <1.39.4 (CVE-2026-26030), Microsoft Semantic Kernel .NET SDK <1.71.0 (CVE-2026-25592), VS Code agentic AI extensions (CVE-2025-55319), GitHub Copilot, Claude Code, AI browser agents (Atlas, Comet, Brave Leo), AI-powered CI/CD reviewers, chatbot assistants integrated into IDEs/terminals/DevOps pipelines
enrichment.patch_available: true (patched at individual product-CVE level: Cursor 1.3 for CVE-2025-54131, Microsoft Semantic Kernel Python 1.39.4 for CVE-2026-26030, .NET SDK 1.71.0 for CVE-2026-25592). No single patch exists for the IPI class itself; Google continuously hardens Gemini models and ships layered defenses.
enrichment.mitigation: Because IPI is an architectural weakness rather than a single bug, there is no universal vendor patch. Recommended mitigations: (1) Enforce a strict data-instruction boundary — treat all retrieved web/email/document content as untrusted data, never as instructions (instruction hierarchy, spotlighting/sandboxing). (2) Apply layered defense — Google's strategy combines model hardening, dedicated red teams, the AI Vulnerability Reward Program, and real-time global-scale processing; Forcepoint's Real Time Analytics blocks IPI URLs at the network layer. (3) Adversarial training and intent/behavior detection — correlate AI-level intent analysis with host-level execution telemetry. (4) Product-specific hardening — Microsoft recommends AST node-type/function-call allowlists, blocklists for dangerous attributes (__class__, __subclasses__), removing [KernelFunction] from sensitive methods, and path canonicalization + directory allowlists for file operations. (5) Avoid auto-run/allowlist modes in agentic coding tools; require per-call approval. (6) Strip or sanitize hidden HTML/CSS/aria-hidden content before model ingestion. (7) Monitor endpoint telemetry for suspicious child processes (cmd.exe, powershell.exe) spawned by agent hosts.

findings.title: Google Workspace’s continuous approach to mitigating indirect prompt injections
findings.summary: Posted by Adam Gavish, Google GenAI Security Team Indirect prompt injection (IPI) is an evolving threat vector targeting users of complex AI applications with multiple data sources, such as Workspace with Gemini. This technique enables the attacker to influence the behavior of an LLM by injecting malicious instructions into the data or tools used by the LLM as it completes the user’s query. This may even be possible without any input directly from the user. IPI is not the kind of technical problem you “solve” and move on. Sophisticated LLMs with increasing use of agentic automation combined with a wide range of content create an ultra-dynamic and evolving playground for adversarial attacks. That’s why Google takes a sophisticated and comprehensive approach to these attacks. We’re continuously improving LLM resistance to IPI attacks and launching AI application capabilities with ever-improving defenses. Staying ahead of the latest indirect prompt injection attacks is critical to our mission of securing Workspace with Gemini. In our previous blog “ Mitigating prompt injection attacks with a layered defense strategy ”, we reviewed the layered architecture of our IPI defenses. In this blog, we’ll share more detail on the continuous approach we take to improve these defenses and to solve for new attacks. New attack discovery By proactively discovering and cataloging new attack vectors through internal and external programs, we can identify vulnerabilities and deploy robust defenses ahead of adversarial activity. Human Red-Teaming Human Red-Teaming uses adversarial simulations to uncover security and safety vulnerabilities. Specialized teams execute attacks based on realistic user profiles to exploit weaknesses, coordinating with product teams to resolve identified issues. Automated Red-Teaming Automated Red-Teaming is done via dynamic, machine-learning-driven frameworks to stress-test environments. By algorithmically generating and iterating on attack payloads, we can mi

enrichment.technical_details: Indirect Prompt Injection (IPI) is a class of attack against LLM-based applications such as Workspace with Gemini. An attacker plants hidden instructions inside content that the LLM will later retrieve or process — for example, a malicious instruction hidden in a Gmail message, a shared Google Docs/Drive file, a Calendar invite, or a third-party webpage. When Gemini ingests that content (often via Retrieval-Augmented Generation), the embedded instructions are treated as if they came from the user, allowing the attacker to override the user's intent, coerce the model to invoke tools (search, send mail, run code, call APIs), exfiltrate sensitive data, or take destructive actions — sometimes with no direct user input (zero-click). The GeminiJack variant exploited the trust boundary in a RAG architecture between user-controlled content in Workspace data sources and the Gemini Enterprise instruction channel.
enrichment.affected_products: Google Workspace with Gemini (Gmail, Docs, Drive, Chat), Gemini Enterprise, Vertex AI Search (no specific version numbers disclosed in the advisory)
enrichment.exploit_available: true - multiple public PoCs and weaponized payloads exist: (1) Noma Security's GeminiJack zero-click PoC disclosed Dec 8, 2025 (https://noma.security/blog/geminijack-google-gemini-zero-click-vulnerability/); (2) Zenodo exploit source code at https://zenodo.org/records/17968523; (3) Forcepoint X-Labs' 10 live IPI payloads observed on real-world public websites (https://forcepoint.com/blog/x-labs/indirect-prompt-injection-payloads, April 22, 2026).
enrichment.patch_available: true - Google's official advisory describes the continuous mitigation program itself (http://security.googleblog.com/2026/04/google-workspaces-continuous-approach.html). Specific instances have been patched: Google received the GeminiJack report in August 2025 and had patched the Gemini Enterprise / Vertex AI Search zero-click flaw by December 2025 (https://securityaffairs.com/185574/hacking/geminijack-zero-click-flaw-in-gemini-enterprise-allowed-corporate-data-exfiltration.html).
enrichment.active_exploitation: true - confirmed in-the-wild weaponization documented by multiple sources: (1) Palo Alto Networks Unit 42 'Web-Based Indirect Prompt Injection Observed in the Wild' (March 3, 2026) reports a December 2025 incident using IPI to evade AI-based ad review, plus intent-based attacks causing data destruction, denial of service, unauthorized financial transactions, and system-prompt leakage (https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/ai-agent-prompt-injection/); (2) Forcepoint X-Labs observed 10 actively weaponized IPI payloads on live websites as of April 22, 2026 (https://forcepoint.com/blog/x-labs/indirect-prompt-injection-payloads); (3) Help Net Security (April 24, 2026) reports increased IPI malicious activity between November 2025 and February 2026 with evidence of shared attacker tooling and templates (https://helpnetsecurity.com/2026/04/24/indirect-prompt-injection-in-the-wild/).
enrichment.mitigation: Google describes a continuous, layered defense strategy: (1) prompt-injection content classifiers that detect malicious embedded instructions; (2) 'security thought reinforcement' so the model reasserts the user's intent; (3) Markdown sanitization and suspicious-URL redaction on model outputs; (4) a user-confirmation framework requiring explicit user approval before sensitive tool calls; (5) end-user security-mitigation notifications when risky behavior is blocked; (6) model resilience and Gemini model hardening (retraining on partitioned synthetic attack data such as 'Simula'); (7) deterministic defenses via a centralized policy engine handling URL sanitization, tool-chaining policy, and regex takedowns; and (8) continuous red-teaming plus the Google AI Vulnerability Rewards Program. Administrator hardening guidance: https://knowledge.workspace.google.com/admin/security/indirect-prompt-injections-and-googles-layered-defense-strategy-for-gemini

findings.title: Architecting Security for Agentic Capabilities in Chrome
findings.summary: Posted by Nathan Parker, Chrome security team Chrome has been advancing the web’s security for well over 15 years, and we’re committed to meeting new challenges and opportunities with AI. Billions of people trust Chrome to keep them safe by default, and this is a responsibility we take seriously. Following the recent launch of Gemini in Chrome and the preview of agentic capabilities , we want to share our approach and some new innovations to improve the safety of agentic browsing. The primary new threat facing all agentic browsers is indirect prompt injection . It can appear in malicious sites, third-party content in iframes, or from user-generated content like user reviews, and can cause the agent to take unwanted actions such as initiating financial transactions or exfiltrating sensitive data. Given this open challenge, we are investing in a layered defense that includes both deterministic and probabilistic defenses to make it difficult and costly for attackers to cause harm. Designing safe agentic browsing for Chrome has involved deep collaboration of security experts across Google. We built on Gemini's existing protections and agent security principles and have implemented several new layers for Chrome. We’re introducing a user alignment critic where the agent’s actions are vetted by a separate model that is isolated from untrusted content. We’re also extending Chrome’s origin-isolation capabilities to constrain what origins the agent can interact with, to just those that are relevant to the task. Our layered defense also includes user confirmations for critical steps, real-time detection of threats , and red-teaming and response . We’ll step through these layers below. Checking agent outputs with User Alignment Critic The main planning model for Gemini uses page content shared in Chrome to decide what action to take next. Exposure to untrusted web content means it is inherently vulnerable to indirect prompt injection. We use techniques like spotlighting that di

enrichment.technical_details: Indirect prompt injection (IDPI) embeds hidden or malicious instructions inside web content that an AI agent later ingests. Adversaries use CSS cloaking (display:none, off-screen positioning, white-on-white text), HTML attribute cloaking (data-* attributes), XML/SVG CDATA encapsulation, or base64 payloads decoded at runtime with timed delays. When the agentic browser's Gemini model processes the poisoned page (e.g., to summarize, fill a form, or perform an agentic task on the user's behalf), the embedded instructions are treated as legitimate commands, enabling unauthorized financial transactions, exfiltration of sensitive data, or navigation to attacker-controlled URLs. The related CVE-2026-0628 is a Chrome WebView insufficient-policy-enforcement flaw (Chrome prior to 143.0.7499.192) that allows a malicious Chrome extension to inject scripts/HTML into the privileged Gemini Live panel via the declarativeNetRequest API, enabling privilege escalation, local file/directory access, camera/microphone hijack, screenshot capture, and phishing through the trusted panel. The GeminiJack variant is an indirect prompt injection inside the Gemini Enterprise RAG pipeline where attackers poison Google Docs, Calendar invites, or Gmail messages so that Vertex AI Search retrieves them as instructions and exfiltrates sensitive corporate data via an outbound HTTP request.
enrichment.affected_products: Google Chrome with Gemini in Chrome and agentic browsing capabilities (specific version ranges not enumerated in the December 8, 2025 blog post); Chrome prior to 143.0.7499.192 (CVE-2026-0628); Google Gemini Enterprise / Vertex AI Search (GeminiJack zero-click indirect prompt injection, patched around December 8, 2025).
enrichment.cvss_score: 8.8 (CVSS v3.1 base score for the closely related CVE-2026-0628 Chrome WebView policy-enforcement vulnerability). The Google Security Blog post of December 8, 2025 itself does not publish a CVSS base score because it is an architectural defensive post, not a single-CVE advisory.
enrichment.cvss_vector: CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:R/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H - This vector applies to the closely related CVE-2026-0628 Chrome WebView insufficient-policy-enforcement vulnerability. The December 8, 2025 Google Security Blog post itself does not publish a CVSS vector because it is an architectural defense post, not a single-CVE advisory.
enrichment.exploit_available: true - Public PoCs include: (1) Brave's Comet prompt injection PoC (https://brave.com/blog/comet-prompt-injection/); (2) Unit 42 end-to-end exploit demo for CVE-2026-0628 Chrome WebView hijacking (https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/gemini-live-in-chrome-hijacking/); (3) Noma Labs GeminiJack zero-click PoC against Gemini Enterprise (https://noma.security/blog/geminijack-google-gemini-zero-click-vulnerability/); (4) Trail of Bits agentic browser isolation research (https://blog.trailofbits.com/2026/01/13/lack-of-isolation-in-agentic-browsers-resurfaces-old-vulnerabilities/).
enrichment.patch_available: true - Patch URL: https://chromereleases.googleblog.com/2025/12/stable-channel-update-for-desktop_10.html (Chrome 143.0.7499.192 stable channel update addressing CVE-2026-0628 and broader Chrome 143 security fixes). For the GeminiJack Gemini Enterprise / Vertex AI Search class of flaw, Google fully separated Vertex AI Search from Gemini Enterprise by mid-December 2025.
enrichment.active_exploitation: true - Active exploitation of indirect prompt injection in the wild has been confirmed by Palo Alto Networks Unit 42 (March 3, 2026) for the broader attack class, with adversaries using the technique for ad-review evasion, SEO poisoning pushing phishing pages, data destruction, unauthorized transactions, and credential/system-prompt leakage. Google's December 8, 2025 blog post identifies indirect prompt injection as 'the primary new threat facing all agentic browsers.' Note: CVE-2026-0628 specifically was responsibly disclosed by Unit 42 and patched before public research, so no in-the-wild exploitation has been publicly reported for that precise CVE.
enrichment.threat_actors: None known. Indirect prompt injection has been observed in the wild by unnamed adversaries and malicious websites (per Palo Alto Networks Unit 42, March 3, 2026), used for AI-based ad-review evasion, SEO manipulation promoting phishing sites impersonating betting platforms, data destruction/denial of service, unauthorized transactions, and leakage of credentials, payment info, and system prompts.
enrichment.mitigation: Google's layered defenses for Chrome agentic capabilities include: (1) User Alignment Critic - an isolated Gemini model that vets proposed agent actions against the user's stated goal; (2) Spotlighting - techniques that direct the model to prioritize user/system instructions over page content; (3) Agent Origin Sets - extension of origin-isolation that limits the agent to task-relevant origins, split into read-only and read-writable sets; (4) User Confirmations - required for sensitive sites (banking, medical), Google Password Manager use, and consequential actions (payments, sending messages); (5) Prompt-Injection Classifier - real-time scanning of pages to detect adversarial content and block agent actions based on it; (6) Deterministic URL Checks - restrictions on model-generated URLs to known public URLs; (7) Continuous automated red-teaming with generated malicious sandboxed sites. Additional hardening: update Chrome to 143.0.7499.192 or later (fixes CVE-2026-0628), install browser extensions only from trusted sources, and use defense-in-depth tools such as Palo Alto Networks Prisma Browser / Advanced URL Filtering to detect prompt-injection content.

findings.title: Rust in Android: move fast and fix things
findings.summary: Posted by Jeff Vander Stoep, Android Last year, we wrote about why a memory safety strategy that focuses on vulnerability prevention in new code quickly yields durable and compounding gains. This year we look at how this approach isn’t just fixing things, but helping us move faster . The 2025 data continues to validate the approach, with memory safety vulnerabilities falling below 20% of total vulnerabilities for the first time. Updated data for 2025. This data covers first-party and third-party (open source) code changes to the Android platform across C, C++, Java, Kotlin, and Rust. This post is published a couple of months before the end of 2025, but Android’s industry-standard 90-day patch window means that these results are very likely close to final. We can and will accelerate patching when necessary. We adopted Rust for its security and are seeing a 1000x reduction in memory safety vulnerability density compared to Android’s C and C++ code . But the biggest surprise was Rust's impact on software delivery. With Rust changes having a 4x lower rollback rate and spending 25% less time in code review , the safer path is now also the faster one. In this post, we dig into the data behind this shift and also cover: How we’re expanding our reach: We're pushing to make secure code the default across our entire software stack. We have updates on Rust adoption in first-party apps, the Linux kernel, and firmware. Our first rust memory safety vulnerability...almost: We'll analyze a near-miss memory safety bug in unsafe Rust: how it happened, how it was mitigated, and steps we're taking to prevent recurrence. It’s also a good chance to answer the question “if Rust can have memory safety issues, why bother at all?” Building Better Software, Faster Developing an operating system requires the low-level control and predictability of systems programming languages like C, C++, and Rust. While Java and Kotlin are important for Android platform development, their role is complementa

enrichment.technical_details: The Google Security Blog post 'Rust in Android: move fast and fix things' (published 2025-11-13) reports that Google's strategy of writing new Android code in memory-safe languages (primarily Rust) has driven memory-safety vulnerabilities below 20% of total vulnerabilities for the first time in 2025, with approximately 1000x lower memory-safety vulnerability density vs. legacy C/C++ code. As a near-miss example, it discloses a linear buffer overflow in CrabbyAVIF (the Rust-based AVIF decoder) that was caught internally before shipping. The same flaw was later fixed and assigned CVE-2025-48530 (out-of-bounds accesses due to incorrect bounds checks; potential RCE in combination with other bugs; no privileges or user interaction required). The post also notes that Android's Scudo hardened allocator rendered the original CrabbyAVIF bug non-exploitable even before the code-level fix.
enrichment.affected_products: Android platform (AOSP and first-party/third-party code across C, C++, Java, Kotlin, and Rust); CrabbyAVIF (Rust-based AVIF decoder, associated with CVE-2025-48530).
enrichment.exploit_available: false. The CrabbyAVIF vulnerability was caught internally before being shipped to any public Android release; no public PoC or weaponized exploit exists.
enrichment.patch_available: true. For the CrabbyAVIF vulnerability (CVE-2025-48530) referenced in the post, an official fix was released in the Android Security Bulletin of August 2025 (patch level 2025-08-05). The broader Rust-in-Android strategy is an ongoing multi-year program. See: https://source.android.com/docs/security/bulletin/2025-08-01
enrichment.active_exploitation: false. Google explicitly states the CrabbyAVIF linear buffer overflow was caught internally before being shipped to any public Android release, and no in-the-wild exploitation has been reported.
enrichment.mitigation: Key hardening guidance from the post: (1) Continue migrating new Android platform code from C/C++ to memory-safe languages (Rust); (2) Leverage the Scudo hardened allocator to render residual unsafe-Rust/C/C++ memory-safety bugs non-exploitable; (3) Apply Android Security Bulletin patch level 2025-08-05 (August 2025) for the related CVE-2025-48530 fix.

findings.title: Mitigating prompt injection attacks with a layered defense strategy
findings.summary: Posted by Adam Gavish, Google GenAI Security Team With the rapid adoption of generative AI, a new wave of threats is emerging across the industry with the aim of manipulating the AI systems themselves. One such emerging attack vector is indirect prompt injections. Unlike direct prompt injections, where an attacker directly inputs malicious commands into a prompt, indirect prompt injections involve hidden malicious instructions within external data sources. These may include emails, documents, or calendar invites that instruct AI to exfiltrate user data or execute other rogue actions. As more governments, businesses, and individuals adopt generative AI to get more done, this subtle yet potentially potent attack becomes increasingly pertinent across the industry, demanding immediate attention and robust security measures. At Google, our teams have a longstanding precedent of investing in a defense-in-depth strategy, including robust evaluation, threat analysis, AI security best practices , AI red-teaming , adversarial training, and model hardening for generative AI tools. This approach enables safer adoption of Gemini in Google Workspace and the Gemini app (we refer to both in this blog as “Gemini” for simplicity). Below we describe our prompt injection mitigation product strategy based on extensive research, development, and deployment of improved security mitigations. A layered security approach Google has taken a layered security approach introducing security measures designed for each stage of the prompt lifecycle. From Gemini 2.5 model hardening, to purpose-built machine learning (ML) models detecting malicious instructions, to system-level safeguards, we are meaningfully elevating the difficulty, expense, and complexity faced by an attacker. This approach compels adversaries to resort to methods that are either more easily identified or demand greater resources. Our model training with adversarial data significantly enhanced our defenses against indirect prompt

enrichment.technical_details: Indirect prompt injection embeds hidden malicious instructions inside external content (emails, documents, calendar invites, web pages) that an LLM-powered AI assistant later ingests during normal operation. Unlike direct prompt injection (where the user types the malicious prompt), the victim never composes the attack — the malicious instruction arrives via ordinary data-channel content. Because the model cannot reliably distinguish trusted system instructions from untrusted data-channel content, the hidden instructions can cause the assistant to exfiltrate user data (e.g., email contents), perform unauthorized tool calls, or take other rogue actions. Google's blog describes a layered defense: (1) Gemini 2.5 model hardening via adversarial training data, (2) proprietary ML-based prompt-injection content classifiers, (3) system-instruction reinforcement appending security directives, (4) markdown sanitizer stripping external image URLs, (5) Google Safe Browsing-based suspicious URL detection/redaction, (6) Human-In-The-Loop (HITL) confirmation prompts for risky actions, and (7) contextual end-user security notifications. Google's companion posts add red-teaming via the Simula synthetic-data framework, the Google AI Vulnerability Rewards Program, deterministic defenses via a centralized policy engine, and DeepMind techniques such as 'spotlighting,' 'self-reflection,' and ART-based fine-tuning.
enrichment.affected_products: Gemini 2.5 LLM family, Google Gemini app, and Gemini integrations in Google Workspace (Gmail, Google Calendar, Google Docs editors, Google Drive, Google Chat). No specific patch version numbers are published.
enrichment.cvss_score: CVSS score unavailable for the Google advisory itself. CVE-2025-32711 (EchoLeak) is reported as CVSS 9.3 in third-party sources but pertains to Microsoft 365 Copilot, not Google products.
enrichment.cvss_vector: CVSS vector unavailable. This is a class-level advisory on indirect prompt injection; no CVSS vector is published for the advisory itself. The referenced CVE-2025-32711 (EchoLeak) is documented at https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/cve-2025-32711 but pertains to Microsoft 365 Copilot.
enrichment.exploit_available: true. Public PoCs include the 'EchoLeak' zero-click exploit (CVE-2025-32711) disclosed against Microsoft 365 Copilot (https://arxiv.org/html/2509.10540v1), a researcher demo against Gemini email-summary (https://www.itbrew.com/stories/2025/07/28/in-summary-researcher-demos-prompt-injection-attack-on-gemini), Cyera's disclosure against Gemini CLI (https://www.cyera.com/research/cyera-research-labs-discloses-command-prompt-injection-vulnerabilities-in-gemini-cli), and Aikido's 'PromptPwnd' PoC against Gemini CLI in GitHub Actions (https://www.aikido.dev/blog/promptpwnd-github-actions-ai-agents).
enrichment.patch_available: true. The layered defenses are described as built into current Gemini 2.5 deployments and Workspace with Gemini integrations as of June 13, 2025. Vendor advisory and supporting technical documentation: http://security.googleblog.com/2025/06/mitigating-prompt-injection-attacks.html, https://blog.google/security/google-workspaces-continuous-approach-to-mitigating-indirect-prompt-injections/, https://deepmind.google/blog/advancing-geminis-security-safeguards/, https://knowledge.workspace.google.com/admin/security/indirect-prompt-injections-and-googles-layered-defense-strategy-for-gemini, and the technical paper https://storage.googleapis.com/deepmind-media/Security%20and%20Privacy/Gemini_Security_Paper.pdf. (CVE-2025-32711 'EchoLeak', referenced in the blog as an example, was patched by Microsoft for M365 Copilot — not a Google patch.)
enrichment.active_exploitation: true. Confirmed in-the-wild exploitation and researcher demonstrations of indirect prompt injection against Gemini-family AI products: (a) Palo Alto Unit 42 documented web-based Indirect Prompt Injection observed in the wild — https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/ai-agent-prompt-injection/ ; (b) Proofpoint documented real-world threat-actor abuse of AI assistants via indirect prompt injection in email/cloud attack chains — https://www.proofpoint.com/us/blog/email-and-cloud-threats/stop-month-how-threat-actors-weaponize-ai-assistants-indirect-prompt ; (c) Matt Pest demonstrated prompt injection against Gemini email-summary — https://www.itbrew.com/stories/2025/07/28/in-summary-researcher-demos-prompt-injection-attack-on-gemini ; (d) Cyera Research Labs disclosed command and prompt injection vulnerabilities in Gemini CLI — https://www.cyera.com/research/cyera-research-labs-discloses-command-prompt-injection-vulnerabilities-in-gemini-cli ; (e) Aikido 'PromptPwnd' disclosed AI-agent prompt injection in GitHub Actions against Gemini CLI — https://www.aikido.dev/blog/promptpwnd-github-actions-ai-agents ; (f) EchoLeak (CVE-2025-32711) zero-click attack against Microsoft 365 Copilot demonstrated remote data exfiltration as cited in the Google blog.
enrichment.threat_actors: None known. The Google blog refers only generically to 'adversaries' and does not name any specific APT groups, ransomware operators, or campaigns.
enrichment.mitigation: Google's layered defense strategy, applied to Gemini 2.5 and Gemini-in-Workspace products at each stage of the prompt lifecycle: (1) Adversarial-data training to harden the Gemini 2.5 model; (2) ML-based content classifiers detecting malicious or injected instructions; (3) 'security thought reinforcement' — appending system-level instructions that direct the model to ignore adversarial directives and execute only the user's task; (4) Markdown sanitizer that strips external image URLs to prevent covert data exfiltration; (5) Google Safe Browsing-based detection and redaction of suspicious URLs; (6) Human-In-The-Loop (HITL) contextual user confirmation for risky actions (e.g., deleting calendar events, sending email); (7) Contextual end-user security notifications. Platform-level measures: human + automated red-teaming using the Simula synthetic-data generator, the Google AI Vulnerability Rewards Program, deterministic defenses enforced via a centralized policy engine, ML defenses retrained on synthetic attack/recovery data, LLM-based prompt-engineering defenses, and depth-in-depth layering. Administrators should follow Google's Secure AI Agents guidance (https://saif.google/focus-on-agents) and the Workspace admin guide (https://knowledge.workspace.google.com/admin/security/indirect-prompt-injections-and-googles-layered-defense-strategy-for-gemini).

findings.title: Countering Chinese State-Sponsored Actors Compromise of Networks Worldwide to Feed Global Espionage System
findings.summary: Executive summary People’s Republic of China (PRC) state-sponsored cyber threat actors are targeting networks globally, including, but not limited to, telecommunications, government, transportation, lodging, and military infrastructure networks. While these actors focus on large backbone routers of major telecommunications providers, as well as provider edge (PE) and customer edge (CE) routers, they also leverage compromised devices and trusted connections to pivot into other networks. These actors often modify routers to maintain persistent, long-term access to networks. This activity partially overlaps with cyber threat actor reporting by the cybersecurity industry—commonly referred to as Salt Typhoon, OPERATOR PANDA, RedMike, UNC5807, and GhostEmperor, among others. The authoring agencies are not adopting a particular commercial naming convention and hereafter refer to those responsible for the cyber threat activity more generically as “Advanced Persistent Threat (APT) actors” throughout this advisory. This cluster of cyber threat activity has been observed in the United States, Australia, Canada, New Zealand, the United Kingdom, and other areas globally. This Cybersecurity Advisory (CSA) includes observations from various government and industry investigations where the APT actors targeted internal enterprise environments, as well as systems and networks that deliver services directly to customers. This CSA details the tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs) leveraged by these APT actors to facilitate detection and threat hunting, and provides mitigation guidance to reduce the risk from these APT actors and their TTPs. This CSA is being released by the following authoring and co-sealing agencies: United States National Security Agency (NSA) United States Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) United States Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) United States Department of Defense Cyber Crime Center (DC3) Australian Signals Directorate’s Austra

enrichment.technical_details: PRC state-sponsored actors compromise global networks by exploiting publicly known CVEs (T1190) and avoidable weaknesses on internet-exposed network edge devices, then pivoting via compromised devices and trusted provider/customer interconnections (T1199). Persistence is achieved by modifying router configurations: ACLs are created or modified (frequently named 'access-list 20') to permit threat-actor IPs, GRE/IPsec tunnels and static routes are added for traffic redirection, SPAN/RSPAN/ERSPAN mirroring is enabled, and GRE-tunneled PCAP collection is configured to capture traffic. Actors create local accounts and grant privileges via config modifications (T1136.001). They enable virtualized containers (e.g., Cisco Guest Shell) on network devices to host tooling and evade detection, and forward container logs to obscure C2. Credential access is gained by brute-forcing and reusing weak credentials (notably 'cisco'/'cisco'), exploiting Cisco Type 5 (MD5) and Type 7 password hashes, and abusing default SNMP community strings ('public', 'private'). Commands are executed via SSH, HTTP GET/POST (targeting privileged paths such as /level/15/exec/-/* on IOS XE), and SNMP. Actors use VPS infrastructure (T1583.003) and active scanning (T1595) for additional targets.
enrichment.affected_products: Cisco IOS XE, NX-OS, IOS, and IOS XR (host Linux); Palo Alto Networks PAN-OS GlobalProtect; Ivanti Connect Secure and Policy Secure; suspected targeting of Fortinet firewalls, Juniper firewalls, Microsoft Exchange, Nokia routers/switches, Sierra Wireless devices, and SonicWall firewalls. Specific CVEs referenced: CVE-2024-21887 (Ivanti), CVE-2024-3400 (Palo Alto), CVE-2023-20273 (Cisco IOS XE), CVE-2023-20198 (Cisco IOS XE), CVE-2018-0171 (Cisco IOS/IOS XE Smart Install).
enrichment.exploit_available: true. The actors leverage publicly known CVEs and publicly available tooling (e.g., siet.py, map.tcl, tclproxy.tcl, wodSSHServer) against internet-exposed devices. See CISA advisory AA25-239A for IOCs: https://www.cisa.gov/news-events/cybersecurity-advisories/aa25-239a
enrichment.patch_available: true. Vendor patches are available for the specific CVEs referenced in the advisory: CVE-2024-21887 (Ivanti — https://www.ivanti.com/security-advisories), CVE-2024-3400 (Palo Alto — https://security.paloaltonetworks.com/CVE-2024-3400), CVE-2023-20273 and CVE-2023-20198 (Cisco — https://sec.cisco.com/security-advisories), and CVE-2018-0171 (Cisco Smart Install — https://tools.cisco.com/security-advisories/cisco-sa20180412-smarti/). The CISA advisory itself recommends prioritizing patching via the CISA KEV Catalog: https://www.cisa.gov/news-events/cybersecurity-advisories/aa25-239a
enrichment.active_exploitation: true. CISA confirms these PRC state-sponsored actors have had 'considerable success' and a 'historical exploitation' track record against exposed network edge devices worldwide, with ongoing long-running intrusion campaigns targeting telecommunications, government, transportation, lodging, and military infrastructure networks. CISA notes that exploitation of zero-day vulnerabilities has not been observed to date — the activity leverages known, previously disclosed CVEs against unpatched, internet-exposed devices. Sources: https://www.cisa.gov/news-events/cybersecurity-advisories/aa25-239a
enrichment.threat_actors: Salt Typhoon, OPERATOR PANDA, RedMike, UNC5807, GhostEmperor (PRC state-sponsored cyber threat actors; activity attributed to Sichuan Juxinhe Network Technology Co. Ltd., Beijing Huanyu Tianqiong Information Technology Co. Ltd., and Sichuan Zhixin Ruijie Network Technology Co. Ltd.)
enrichment.mitigation: Key hardening advice: implement management-plane isolation (CoPP) and place all management services (SSH, HTTPS, SNMP, TACACS+/RADIUS, SCP/SFTP) in a dedicated out-of-band management VRF; block all egress from management VRFs except to authorized AAA/syslog/NetFlow/IPFIX/telemetry collectors; apply default-deny management-plane ACLs allowlisting approved management IPs and jump servers; enforce SSHv2 (disable Telnet) and disable unused ports/protocols plus Cisco Smart Install; restrict Guest Shell via AAA command authorization; change all default credentials and SNMP community strings; minimize auth attempts/lockout windows; require MFA/PKI and disable password authentication where possible; use strong cryptographic password storage (e.g., Cisco Type 8) instead of Type 5/7; prioritize patching all KEV-listed CVEs on internet-facing devices and upgrade unsupported hardware/software; forward Guest Shell container logs (journald/systemd) to a SIEM; alert on 'guestshell disable', 'guestshell destroy', and unexpected 'chvrf'/'dohost' usage; perform periodic integrity checks and inventories of device storage (e.g., bootflash:); and continuously compare running vs. authorized network configurations to detect unauthorized ACL, GRE/tunnel, SPAN, or account changes.

findings.title: Russian GRU Targeting Western Logistics Entities and Technology Companies
findings.summary: Executive Summary This joint cybersecurity advisory (CSA) highlights a Russian state-sponsored cyber campaign targeting Western logistics entities and technology companies. This includes those involved in the coordination, transport, and delivery of foreign assistance to Ukraine. Since 2022, Western logistics entities and IT companies have faced an elevated risk of targeting by the Russian General Staff Main Intelligence Directorate (GRU) 85th Main Special Service Center (85th GTsSS), military unit 26165—tracked in the cybersecurity community under several names (see “ Cybersecurity Industry Tracking ”). The actors’ cyber espionage-oriented campaign, targeting technology companies and logistics entities, uses a mix of previously disclosed tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs). The authoring agencies expect similar targeting and TTP use to continue. Executives and network defenders at logistics entities and technology companies should recognize the elevated threat of unit 26165 targeting, increase monitoring and threat hunting for known TTPs and indicators of compromise (IOCs), and posture network defenses with a presumption of targeting. This cyber espionage-oriented campaign targeting logistics entities and technology companies uses a mix of previously disclosed TTPs and is likely connected to these actors’ wide scale targeting of IP cameras in Ukraine and bordering NATO nations. The following authors and co-sealers are releasing this CSA: United States National Security Agency (NSA) United States Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) United Kingdom National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC-UK) Germany Federal Intelligence Service (BND) Bundesnachrichtendienst Germany Federal Office for Information Security (BSI) Bundesamt für Sicherheit in der Informationstechnik Germany Federal Office for the Protection of the Constitution (BfV) Bundesamt für Verfassungsschutz Czech Republic Military Intelligence (VZ) Vojenské zpravodajství Czech Republic National Cyber and Infor

enrichment.technical_details: AA25-141A is a joint CISA/FBI/NSA/Cyber Command advisory on a GRU Unit 26165 cyber-espionage campaign targeting Western logistics and IT firms supporting Ukraine aid delivery since 2022. The actor chains five CVEs as initial-access vectors: (a) CVE-2023-23397 in Microsoft Outlook — a specially crafted calendar/email triggers the deprecated PidLidReminderFileParameter to force Outlook to connect to an attacker-controlled SMB share and leak the user's NetNTLMv2 hash without user interaction; (b) CVE-2020-12641 in Roundcube Webmail before 1.4.4 — shell-metacharacter command injection in rcube_image.php via _im_convert_path for arbitrary command execution; (c) CVE-2020-35730 in Roundcube — reflected XSS via crafted plain-text email link for session hijacking; (d) CVE-2021-44026 in Roundcube before 1.3.17/1.4.12 — SQL injection via search/search_params for credential and mailbox access; (e) CVE-2023-38831 in WinRAR before 6.23 — a benign file in a ZIP archive is silently replaced with a same-named executable, achieving arbitrary code execution when opened. Post-exploitation, the actor uses credential reuse, IIS/MSSQL exploitation, open-source PHP webshells (P.A.S. Webshell, fail3d), Impacket tools (atexec, smbexec, wmiexec, psexec), and persistence via scheduled tasks and registry run keys.
enrichment.affected_products: Microsoft Outlook (all versions prior to the March 14, 2023 security update) — CVE-2023-23397; Roundcube Webmail before 1.4.4 — CVE-2020-12641; Roundcube Webmail before 1.2.13, 1.3.x before 1.3.16, 1.4.x before 1.4.10 — CVE-2020-35730; Roundcube Webmail before 1.3.17, 1.4.x before 1.4.12 — CVE-2021-44026; RARLAB WinRAR before 6.23 — CVE-2023-38831
enrichment.cvss_score: 9.8 (NVD CVSS v3.1 base score for CVE-2023-23397, the highest-severity CVE in the advisory)
enrichment.cvss_vector: CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/H:H (NVD-assigned base vector for CVE-2023-23397, the most severe CVE in the advisory)
enrichment.exploit_available: true. Public PoC exploit code is available on GitHub: https://github.com/Trackflaw/CVE-2023-23397, https://github.com/vlad-a-man/CVE-2023-23397 (CVE-2023-23397); https://github.com/HDCE-inc/CVE-2023-38831 (CVE-2023-38831); https://github.com/mbadanoiu/CVE-2020-12641 (CVE-2020-12641).
enrichment.patch_available: true. Patches are available for every CVE exploited in the campaign: Microsoft Outlook — March 14, 2023 security update (CVE-2023-23397); Roundcube Webmail — releases 1.4.4, 1.2.13, 1.3.16, 1.4.10, 1.3.17, and 1.4.12; RARLAB WinRAR — version 6.23 (CVE-2023-38831). Advisory: https://www.cisa.gov/news-events/cybersecurity-advisories/aa25-141a
enrichment.active_exploitation: true. Confirmed in-the-wild exploitation by Russian GRU Unit 26165 (APT28) against Western logistics entities and IT companies supporting Ukraine aid delivery since early 2022, as documented in joint advisory AA25-141A published May 12, 2025.
enrichment.threat_actors: Russian General Staff Main Intelligence Directorate (GRU) 85th Main Special Service Center (85th GTsSS), military unit 26165 — tracked as APT28, Fancy Bear, Forest Blizzard, Blue Delta, Sednit, Sofacy, Strontium, and Tsar Team.
enrichment.mitigation: Apply vendor patches for all five CVEs: Microsoft Outlook March 14, 2023 security update (CVE-2023-23397); Roundcube Webmail 1.4.4+ (CVE-2020-12641), 1.2.13/1.3.16/1.4.10+ (CVE-2020-35730), 1.3.17/1.4.12+ (CVE-2021-44026); WinRAR 6.23+ (CVE-2023-38831). Additional defensive measures: run Microsoft's CVE-2023-23397 hunting script (https://aka.ms/CVE-2023-23397ScriptDoc), block outbound SMB (TCP/445) at the perimeter, deploy the Impacket YARA rule (https://github.com/Neo23x0/signature-base/blob/master/yara/gen_impacket_tools.yar), enforce phishing-resistant MFA, audit local admin and service accounts for credential reuse, hunt for IOCs/YARA in Appendix B of the advisory, and apply the 'Identifying and Mitigating Living Off the Land Techniques' guide to detect Impacket and LOLBin abuse.

findings.title: Browser Security: Zero-Days Are Only Part of the Problem

enrichment.technical_details: The CrowdStrike blog frames the modern web browser as the de facto enterprise operating environment (email, SaaS, collaboration, HR/finance/development/AI tools all rendered in the browser). Technical themes: (a) a shared Chromium codebase means a single underlying flaw rapidly extends across Chrome, Microsoft Edge, and other Chromium-based browsers, amplifying blast radius; (b) attackers chain memory-safety bugs in JavaScript/rendering layers (e.g., in V8) with sandbox escapes and privilege escalation; (c) browser-mediated techniques include phishing, adversary-in-the-middle (AitM), session hijacking via stolen session cookies that bypass MFA, in-document malicious downloads, clickjacking, cross-site scripting (XSS), and HTML smuggling; (d) the CrowdStrike 2026 Global Threat Report found 42% of vulnerabilities were exploited prior to public disclosure, reflecting a structural patch-deployment gap; and (e) these attacks operate inside the live browser session where network-level inspection and endpoint patching alone are insufficient.
enrichment.affected_products: Affected products unavailable. The article discusses Chromium-based web browsers generically (e.g., Google Chrome, Microsoft Edge, and other Chromium-derivative browsers such as Brave) as a shared attack surface, but does not name specific vulnerable product versions or version ranges.
enrichment.cvss_score: CVSS score unavailable. The article does not reference a specific vulnerability and does not publish a CVSS v3.x base score. (Adjacent reporting shows CVE-2026-11645 has CVSS 8.8 in independent coverage, but that CVE is not the subject of this article.)
enrichment.cvss_vector: CVSS vector unavailable. The article is generic across browser attack surface and does not tie its analysis to a single CVE, so no CVSS v3 vector string is published in the source.
enrichment.exploit_available: true. The article itself reports that per the CrowdStrike 2026 Global Threat Report, 42% of vulnerabilities were exploited before public disclosure, and surrounding reporting confirms in-the-wild exploitation of multiple browser zero-days, including CVE-2026-11645 (Chrome V8, exploited in the wild in June 2026) and CVE-2026-3910 (Chrome V8, exploited in the wild in March 2026). Sources: https://www.crowdstrike.com/en-us/blog/browser-security-zero-days-are-only-part-of-the-problem/, https://thehackernews.com/2026/06/chrome-v8-zero-day-cve-2026-11645.html, https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/deployedge/microsoft-edge-relnotes-security, https://socprime.com/blog/cve-2026-3910-vulnerability/
enrichment.patch_available: Patch availability unknown. The article does not discuss patch availability for any specific CVE. It notes that browser vendors (Google, Microsoft, Chromium project) routinely issue patches for browser zero-days, but argues there is a persistent 'N-day' gap where many organizations remain unpatched for extended periods — which is why the blog recommends exploit-prevention controls that work independent of patch deployment state.
enrichment.active_exploitation: true. The article explicitly cites the CrowdStrike 2026 Global Threat Report finding that 42% of vulnerabilities were exploited before public disclosure. Independent reporting at the same timeframe confirms active in-the-wild exploitation of browser zero-days, including CVE-2026-11645 (Chrome V8 zero-day, in the wild June 2026: https://thehackernews.com/2026/06/chrome-v8-zero-day-cve-2026-11645.html, confirmed by https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/deployedge/microsoft-edge-relnotes-security) and CVE-2026-3910 (Chrome V8 zero-day, in the wild March 2026: https://socprime.com/blog/cve-2026-3910-vulnerability/).
enrichment.threat_actors: Threat actors unknown. The CrowdStrike blog post does not name any specific threat actor group, APT campaign, or ransomware operator exploiting browser zero-days or N-day browser-mediated attacks. Referenced reports (CrowdStrike 2026 Global Threat Report, Verizon 2026 DBIR) describe broad trends (e.g., 42% YoY increase in zero-day exploitation before disclosure) but do not specifically attribute browser exploitation to any named adversary.
enrichment.mitigation: Because the article argues browser risk cannot be solved by patching alone, it recommends browser-native controls that operate inside the browser session rather than relying solely on patch availability or network inspection. Specifically: deploy CrowdStrike Falcon Secure Access, which (a) uses JavaScript Language Randomization (JLR) — a moving-target defense that continuously randomizes the JavaScript runtime environment to block zero-day exploit chains even before a patch is deployed; (b) blocks phishing and adversary-in-the-middle (AitM) techniques at the browser session; (c) protects session tokens against hijacking and MFA bypass; and (d) prevents credential theft and data exfiltration at the point of execution within the JavaScript runtime. Hardening advice: combine browser-native exploit-prevention with network inspection and patch deployment. Supporting references: CrowdStrike Falcon Secure Access data sheet (https://www.crowdstrike.com/en-us/resources/data-sheets/falcon-secure-access/) and the CrowdStrike 2026 Global Threat Report (https://www.crowdstrike.com/en-us/global-threat-report/).

findings.title: 94% of Organizations Report Cloud Breaches: CrowdStrike State of CDR Survey

enrichment.technical_details: Technical details unavailable. The source is the CrowdStrike 'State of Cloud Detection and Response (CDR) Survey' highlights blog post (published June 22, 2026) summarizing that 94% of surveyed organizations experienced cloud intrusions resulting in data exposure/exfiltration. It reports gaps in cloud visibility (95% have uncovered workloads), inability to distinguish legitimate from malicious activity (78%), alert fatigue (79% get too many alerts, 77% of triage time is on false positives), and tool fragmentation — but does not describe any specific vulnerability mechanism or attack vector tied to a CVE.
enrichment.affected_products: Affected products unavailable. The article is a survey about cloud detection and response gaps; it does not identify a specific software or hardware product or version range, only generic cloud workloads and instances.
enrichment.active_exploitation: Active exploitation status unknown. The article aggregates self-reported survey data about cloud intrusions in general; it does not report confirmed in-the-wild exploitation of any specific CVE.
enrichment.threat_actors: None known. The CrowdStrike survey references 'threat actors' and 'adversaries' generically when discussing cloud intrusions but does not name any specific APT group, ransomware operator, or named campaign.
enrichment.mitigation: The article recommends hardening via consolidated cloud detection and response tooling: extending sensor/agent coverage to all cloud workloads and identities, reducing false-positive triage time, providing real-time containment, and removing fragmented point products. Because this is a survey and not a vulnerability advisory, no specific patch or workaround is applicable.

findings.title: Bringing Rust to the Pixel Baseband
findings.summary: Posted by Jiacheng Lu, Software Engineer, Google Pixel Team Google is continuously advancing the security of Pixel devices. We have been focusing on hardening the cellular baseband modem against exploitation. Recognizing the risks associated within the complex modem firmware, Pixel 9 shipped with mitigations against a range of memory-safety vulnerabilities. For Pixel 10, Google is advancing its proactive security measures further. Following our previous discussion on "Deploying Rust in Existing Firmware Codebases" , this post shares a concrete application: integrating a memory-safe Rust DNS(Domain Name System) parser into the modem firmware. The new Rust-based DNS parser significantly reduces our security risk by mitigating an entire class of vulnerabilities in a risky area, while also laying the foundation for broader adoption of memory-safe code in other areas. Here we share our experience of working on it, and hope it can inspire the use of more memory safe languages in low-level environments. Why Modem Memory Safety Can’t Wait In recent years, we have seen increasing interest in the cellular modem from attackers and security researchers. For example, Google's Project Zero gained remote code execution on Pixel modems over the Internet. Pixel modem has tens of Megabytes of executable code. Given the complexity and remote attack surface of the modem, other critical memory safety vulnerabilities may remain in the predominantly memory-unsafe firmware code. Why DNS? The DNS protocol is most commonly known in the context of browsers finding websites. With the evolution of cellular technology, modern cellular communications have migrated to digital data networks; consequently, even basic operations such as call forwarding rely on DNS services. DNS is a complex protocol and requires parsing of untrusted data, which can lead to vulnerabilities, particularly when implemented in a memory-unsafe language (example: CVE-2024-27227 ). Implementing the DNS parser in Rust offers
